text: Ah, wicked. If ever there was a movie whose promo tour seemed specifically designed to turn people away from the end product, it was this. From a tiny, terrified, and weirdly infantilized looking Ariana Grande to the emaciated demon monster of Cynthia Arivo sucking the very life force out of her, the two stars of this film were bad enough on their own. But watching them desperately fawn and clutch at each other like tormented PS so physically and emotionally ruined the everyday noises

and simple interview questions seem to trigger horrifying bouts of PTSD. The question I found myself asking was, "My god, what the hell did this movie do to these two women? And is it going to do the same to me?" That being the case, I should probably get on with this review before I start wasting away and getting terrified of household objects. I said in my review of Wicked Part One that even if I'm not the target audience for this franchise, I still thought it was a

competent and visually impressive movie that stayed true to the source material, such as it is, that made the most of its two leads and their obvious musical abilities. And that if you like the stage production, you were probably going to love the film. And now that I've seen part two, I want to tell you that that opinion has completely stayed exactly the same, which I know isn't the bombastic praise or devastating criticism you were probably hoping for, but stick with me on this because

Wicked's devotion to the source material turns out to be both a blessing and a curse in this case. Anyway, if you'll give me just a few Tik Toks, I'll illuminate you on the plots. The movie picks up sometime after the events of the first film. Alphaba is now public enemy number one in the Land of Oz, renamed the Wicked Witch of the West, and fueled by a propaganda campaign against her by the Wizard and Michelle Yo. And yeah, I know her character probably has a name, but if she's

determined not to act in this movie, then I shall treat her as such. But more on that later. Meanwhile, Glenda the Good is now the smiling public face of the new regime and is busy enjoying all the trappings of fame and popularity that she always dreamed of. She's even engaged to Fiero now, even though he's totally got the hots for Alphaba, but whatever. What can I say? She must have [ __ ] amazing banter. Matters come to a head when Alphaba tries to reconcile

with the wizard, only to discover he's keeping all the animals of Oz imprisoned. So, she frees them and ruins Glenda's wedding in the process. So, Glenda turns against her and suggests using her sister Nessa to lure her out. So, naturally, Michelle Yo does the sensible thing and summons up a tornado that sucks a house all the way from Kansas and lands it right on Nessa's [ __ ] head. [sighs] Boy, that escalated quickly. >> And guess who happens to be on boards? >> Toto,

I have a feeling we're not in Kansas anymore. >> Now, this is the point where Wicked has to awkwardly try and dovetail its story and characters with the events of Wizard of Oz. And funnily enough, it's also the point where the narrative starts to go off the rails a bit. Part of the problem here is the sheer amount of contrived [ __ ] the script has to cram in to explain everything that happens in the original movie. the creation of Tin Man, the Scarecrow and the Cowardly Lion, the

Wicked Witch of the East and the Ruby Slippers, the construction of the yellow brick road, Alphaba's turn to evil and the abandoned castle she lives in with the flying monkeys, Dorothy's arrival at the Emerald City, her interactions with the wizard, and her imprisonment by the Wicked Witch, and even the final battle where she gets uh melted. The movie has to account for all of this stuff in barely half the runtime of the original Wizard of Oz. And it's in such a rust

not just to set up and explain everything, but also recontextualize it all from Alphaba's point of view that it ends up tripping over its own feet half the time. It's a jarring change of pace for a narrative that was fairly well constructed up to that point. And it feels exactly like what it is. A whole other movie that suddenly comes crashing in to spoil the fun. A movie with its own demands, its own rules, and its own events and characters that all have to

be accommodated in a very short space of time. The stage musical suffered from much the same problem, but it was able to neatly sidestep it by treating its source material with the kind of wink wink tongue-in-cheek humor it deserved. It was under no illusions that it was anything other than a slightly silly piece of fanfiction, leeching off a much more iconic and wellestablished story, but it was willing to laugh at itself and invited the audience to just enjoy

it for what it was. And it worked. The problem with the movie version is that it doesn't have that same level of self-awareness or modesty. It's presented more like a legitimate narrative in its own right, just as valid as the real thing. The big dramatic moments are played out for real, like you're expected to believe this is the actual untold story of The Wizard of Oz. And I think that's the film's one weak point, getting ideas above its station and taken itself too

seriously. And it's a shame because in most other respects, Wicked for Good is a pretty fun ride. The big musical numbers are all present and correct. The two leads can belt them out like their lives depend on it. And yeah, the film adds a couple of additional tracks that feel a bit anemic compared to the original material, but whatever. It doesn't spoil anything. The production design and the visual style are just as vibrant and colorful as before, and there's a neat little tonal shift that

happens whenever Dorothy shows up. The color gradient's got an almost technicolor look. The camera angles switch to more static old school cinematography. And what can I say? I just appreciate little details like that. Performances are just as good or as bad as last time. You can tell that Cynthia Arivo and Ariana Grande are good actors because they manage to not act like crazy people for a couple of hours. They look healthier and less skeletal than in real life, and they obviously

have good chemistry together because they're one step away from being surgically grafted onto each other at this point. Yeah, if I was going to be super critical, I could point out that Glenda is actually a selfish, opportunistic [ __ ] for most of the movie, a conspirator in at least one murder, and a possible war criminal who knowingly supports and maintains an oppressive dictatorship because they give her nice shiny things in return, and never really has to face any

consequences for her actions. But never mind. Let's just not think about it too much. You sure about that? You sure about that? That's probably why Michelle Yo is such a jarring counterpoint to them. I hate to say this because I really like her as an actress, but holy [ __ ] there's a couple of uncomfortable truths we need to come to terms with here. One, she absolutely can't sing. And two, she really struggles with the English language. Two pretty major problems when you're

supposed to be playing a scheming, charismatic villain in a musical. I mean, she's not quite Galgadot levels of log cabin acting, but she's also not far off, and I can't shake the feeling that she was badly miscast in this one. Like I said at the start of this review, Wicked for Good is one of those perfect if you like that sort of thing movies. Narratively, it's a bit more chaotic and less streamlined than its predecessor, and the ending's probably going to feel

a bit anticlimactic compared to the epic conflict it seemed to be setting up, but the core story still delivers on everything the stage show did. So, if you're a fan of part one or if you loved the musical and you want to see what it would look like on the big screen, you're probably going to come away from Wicked for Good feeling pretty satisfied. If not, then I don't imagine you'd even bother watching it in the first place. So, what the hell are you even watching this review for? Anyway,
if you'll excuse me, I've got a sudden urge to have a cheeseburger.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today. Go away now.
